evidence: tests/production/test_security_compliance.py:127 — `security_manager.resolve_privileged_pod_violation = AsyncMock(return_value=mock_resolution)` then line 130 `resolution = await security_manager.resolve_privileged_pod_violation(security_context)`. Pattern repeated at lines 255, 376, 505, 651, 762, 897.
diagnosis: Every test in this file mocks the method it is testing onto the manager object and then calls that same mock, so the tests verify mock round-trips rather than any real behavior; no production code path is exercised.
proposed_fix: Replace AsyncMock self-assignment pattern with actual calls to SecurityManager methods. Add a real (or subprocess-stubbed) k8s client in conftest security_manager fixture. Each test should call `await security_manager.<real_method>(context)` and assert on the structure that the real implementation returns, not on a hand-crafted dict.
test_plan: Run pytest tests/production/test_security_compliance.py with no monkeypatching of the method under test; all 25 tests should hit real SecurityManager code and pass or fail meaningfully. Confirm with coverage that SecurityManager methods appear in the coverage report.

evidence: No watchdog implementation exists anywhere in src/; grep for 'watchdog', 'heal', 'five_min' returns zero results in src/.
diagnosis: The five-minute watchdog behavior described in the PRD does not exist; there is no loop, scheduler, or health-check reconciler in the codebase.
proposed_fix: Add `src/watchdog.py` with class `FiveMinuteWatchdog`. Data model: `WatchdogState { last_run: datetime, issues_found: list[str], issues_resolved: list[str] }`. Interface: `async def run_cycle(k8s_manager, diagnostics_manager) -> WatchdogState`. Cycle: (1) call `diagnostics_manager.diagnose_cluster_health()`, (2) for each warning compare against previous state, (3) attempt auto-remediation via `k8s_manager.execute_remediation()`, (4) re-run health check to confirm resolution (readiness probe passes, zero new warnings). Schedule via `asyncio.create_task` with 300s sleep in `mcp_server.start()`.
test_plan: Unit test: inject a mock diagnostics_manager that returns a warning on first call and clean on second; assert WatchdogState.issues_resolved contains the warning. Integration test: run two cycles against a real (kind) cluster with a broken pod; verify pod is remediated by cycle 2.

evidence: VSCODE_K8S_INTEGRATION.md:56 — `curl http://172.100.10.107:30001/health`; VSCODE_K8S_INTEGRATION.md:244 — `{"status":"healthy",...}` success indicator. The active entry point src/mcp_server.py uses stdio transport (line 640: `async with stdio_server()`) with no HTTP server or /health route; src/main.py has a /health route at line 80 but that file is not the active entry point.
diagnosis: The doc claims a /health HTTP endpoint accessible via NodePort and curl, but the production entry point (mcp_server.py) is stdio-only with no HTTP listener, making those instructions false and the stated success indicators unreachable.
proposed_fix: Either (a) update VSCODE_K8S_INTEGRATION.md to remove all curl /health references and replace Method 1 NodePort section with a note that the server is stdio-only (no HTTP port), or (b) wire src/main.py (which has the HTTP server) as the real entry point, add a /health handler that queries the cluster, and update the doc to reference main.py. Hardcoded IP 172.100.10.107 must also be replaced with a placeholder.
test_plan: After fix (a): verify the doc contains no curl commands pointing to /health. After fix (b): `curl http://<node-ip>:30001/health` returns 200 with `{"status":"healthy"}` against a real cluster.

evidence: tests/unit/test_issue_patterns.py:52-53 — `github_issues_manager.analyze_issue_pattern = AsyncMock(return_value=mock_result)` then `result = await github_issues_manager.analyze_issue_pattern(pattern)`. Pattern repeated at lines 93, 129, 160, 191, etc. through all 20 async tests.
diagnosis: All async tests in this file assign AsyncMock to the method being tested on the fixture object, then call that mock, so nothing in GitHubIssuesManager is exercised; the tests are tautological.
proposed_fix: Remove all `github_issues_manager.<method> = AsyncMock(...)` assignments inside test bodies. The conftest already populates `manager.db_path = ':memory:'` and calls `populate_test_issues`. Write each test to call the real method (e.g. `await github_issues_manager.find_similar_issues(...)`) and assert the returned dict has the expected keys and plausible values. For methods not yet implemented, mark the test `@pytest.mark.xfail(strict=True)` until implemented.
test_plan: Run pytest tests/unit/test_issue_patterns.py -v; confirm tests exercise real GitHubIssuesManager code paths; check coverage shows github_issues_manager.py lines covered.

evidence: src/diagnostics_manager.py does not contain pattern-matching logic for ImagePullBackOff, CrashLoopBackOff, OOMKilled, or probe failures; grep for those strings returns zero hits in src/diagnostics_manager.py.
diagnosis: PodAnalyzer behavior for the four key failure modes does not exist; troubleshoot_pod_issues is a stub that returns canned data rather than analyzing real pod state.
proposed_fix: Add `src/pod_analyzer.py` with `class PodAnalyzer`. Interface: `async def analyze(pod_name, namespace, k8s_client) -> PodAnalysis`. Detection: (1) call `k8s_client.read_namespaced_pod_status()`, inspect `container_statuses[*].state.waiting.reason` for `ImagePullBackOff`/`CrashLoopBackOff`; (2) inspect `lastState.terminated.reason == 'OOMKilled'`; (3) inspect events for `Unhealthy` reason to detect probe failures. Return `PodAnalysis { issue_type, severity, evidence_lines, suggested_fix }`. Wire into DiagnosticsManager.troubleshoot_pod_issues.
test_plan: Unit tests with mocked k8s_client responses covering all four failure modes; assert correct issue_type and non-empty suggested_fix. Integration test against a kind cluster with a deliberately broken pod.

evidence: src/enhanced_tools.py:11-42 — `Tool(name='kubectl_get', ...)` defined. Grep of src/mcp_server.py for 'enhanced_tools' returns zero results; grep for 'kubectl_get' in src/mcp_server.py also returns zero results.
diagnosis: The kubectl_get tool schema is defined in enhanced_tools.py but enhanced_tools.py is never imported by mcp_server.py, so the tool is never registered or callable by any MCP client.
proposed_fix: In src/mcp_server.py add `from enhanced_tools import get_enhanced_kubectl_tools`. In `handle_list_tools`, extend the returned `tools` list: `tools.extend(get_enhanced_kubectl_tools())`. In `handle_call_tool`, add `elif name == 'kubectl_get': result = await self.kubectl_manager.get(resource=arguments['resource'], name=arguments.get('name'), namespace=arguments.get('namespace'), output_format=arguments.get('output_format','yaml'))`. Confirm KubectlManager.get() calls the real k8s API via subprocess or client.
test_plan: Start the MCP server; call list_tools and verify 'kubectl_get' appears in the response. Call kubectl_get with resource='pods' against a live cluster and verify a non-empty pod list is returned.

evidence: src/mcp_server.py:566-569 — `elif name == 'get_best_practices': result = await self.documentation_manager.get_best_practices(category=arguments.get('category'))`. src/documentation_manager.py:376-380 — `async def get_best_practices(self, category=None)` returns from `self.best_practices`, which is a hardcoded dict loaded at line 281-326 in `_load_best_practices()`. No k8s API call is made.
diagnosis: get_best_practices returns a static hardcoded dictionary that was never populated from any real cluster state or live documentation fetch; it does not reflect the actual cluster being managed.
proposed_fix: Keep the static fallback but add a live-documentation layer: in `DocumentationManager.initialize()`, after `_load_best_practices()`, call `_build_documentation_index()` (already implemented) and merge scraped best practices into `self.best_practices`. Additionally, add a method that intersects best practices with real cluster state (e.g., flag which practices the live cluster violates) by querying k8s_manager. Surface those violations in the tool response.
test_plan: Call get_best_practices with category='security'; verify the response includes items scraped from kubernetes.io/docs (not only the 6 hardcoded strings). Separately call without a cluster and verify graceful fallback to static list.

evidence: src/k8s_manager.py:29-33 — `async def initialize(self): ... # Kubernetes client initialization would go here / # For now, we'll use a stub implementation`. src/k8s_manager.py:49-83 — `get_cluster_info` returns hardcoded values: `"version": "v1.29.0"`, `"total": 3`, `"ready": 3`, `"total": 45`, `"running": 42`.
diagnosis: get_cluster_info returns entirely fabricated cluster data; no k8s API client is initialized or called, so every consumer of this tool receives fake metrics regardless of actual cluster state.
proposed_fix: In `KubernetesManager.initialize()`, load kubeconfig via `kubernetes.config.load_kube_config()` (or `load_incluster_config()`) and instantiate `self.v1 = kubernetes.client.CoreV1Api()`, `self.apps_v1 = kubernetes.client.AppsV1Api()`. In `get_cluster_info()`, replace all hardcoded values with: `nodes = self.v1.list_node()`, `pods = self.v1.list_pod_for_all_namespaces()`, `namespaces = self.v1.list_namespace()`, then compute real counts. Return `version` from `self.v1.get_api_versions()`.
test_plan: Run against a kind cluster; assert returned node count matches `kubectl get nodes --no-headers | wc -l`. Assert version string matches `kubectl version --short`. Run with no cluster available and verify a clear error (not fabricated data) is returned.

evidence: No file named `network_policy_analyzer.py` or similar exists in src/; grep for 'NetworkPolicy\|orphan\|denied_flow' in src/ returns zero hits in any manager file.
diagnosis: NetworkPolicyAnalyzer (orphan policies, denied flows) does not exist; no implementation covers this behavior anywhere in the codebase.
proposed_fix: Add `src/network_policy_analyzer.py` with `class NetworkPolicyAnalyzer`. Interface: `async def analyze(namespace, k8s_client) -> NetworkPolicyReport`. Orphan detection: fetch all NetworkPolicy objects via `networking_v1.list_namespaced_network_policy()`; for each, check if any pod in the namespace matches its `podSelector`; policies with no matching pods are orphans. Denied-flow detection: list pods and cross-reference their labels against all ingress/egress rules; flag pod pairs that have no matching allow rule. Return `NetworkPolicyReport { orphan_policies: list, potential_denied_flows: list, coverage_percent: float }`.
test_plan: Unit test with a mock k8s client that returns one policy with a selector matching no pods; assert it appears in orphan_policies. Unit test with two pods and a deny-all policy; assert the pair appears in potential_denied_flows.

evidence: src/k8s_manager.py:166-210 — `execute_remediation` is a stub with only `pod_restart_loop` and `node_not_ready` branches; no probe-tuning logic exists. Grep for 'probe\|initialDelaySeconds\|connection.refused' in src/ returns zero substantive hits.
diagnosis: Deterministic probe-tuning remediation for connection-refused-on-startup does not exist; execute_remediation has no branch for probe failures and applies no actual patch.
proposed_fix: Add a `probe_failure` branch in `execute_remediation`. Logic: (1) fetch pod spec via `v1.read_namespaced_pod()`; (2) identify the failing container's liveness/readiness probe; (3) read current `initialDelaySeconds`; (4) compute new value as `max(current * 2, 30)`; (5) if not dry_run, patch the owning Deployment via `apps_v1.patch_namespaced_deployment()` with the updated probe config; (6) wait for rollout and verify probe passes within 2 minutes. Return `{ old_delay, new_delay, patched_resource, rollout_status }`.
test_plan: Unit test: mock a pod with `initialDelaySeconds=5` and a Unhealthy event; assert patched value equals 10. Integration test: deploy a pod with a probe that fires too early; call execute_remediation; verify pod reaches Running state and probe succeeds.
